---
title: "example_survival"
format: html
editor: source
---


```{r}
# 1. Install 'pacman' only if it is not already installed on your system
if (!require("pacman")) install.packages("pacman")

# 2. Use pacman to install (if missing) and load all required packages
pacman::p_load(
  survival,    # Core survival analysis algorithms and datasets
  survminer,   # Elegant, publication-ready ggplot2-based survival plots
  gtsummary,   # Publication-ready summary and regression tables
  tidycmprsk,  # Modern, tidy framework for competing risks (Fine-Gray)
  mstate,      # Advanced multi-state modelling and data preparation
  coxme,        # Mixed-effects Cox models for shared frailty/clustering
  ggsurvfit    # More, publication-ready ggplot2-based survival plots
)

# 3. Verify packages are loaded
print("All workshop packages successfully loaded!")
```

```{r}
# Load the built-in lung cancer dataset
data(lung)

# 1. Standard Kaplan-Meier
km_fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung)

# Publication-ready KM Plot
ggsurvplot(
  km_fit, 
  data = lung, 
  risk.table = TRUE,       # Adds a risk table below the plot
  pval = TRUE,             # Adds log-rank p-value
  conf.int = TRUE,         # Adds confidence intervals
  palette = c("#E7B800", "#2E9FDF"), # Custom colors
  ggtheme = theme_minimal(), # Clean background
  title = "Overall Survival by Sex (Lung Cancer)",
  legend.labs = c("Male", "Female")
)

# 2. Standard Cox Proportional Hazards Model
cox_fit <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + factor(sex), data = lung)

# Publication-ready Regression Table
tbl_regression(cox_fit, exponentiate = TRUE) %>%
  add_global_p() %>%
  bold_p(t = 0.05) %>%
  bold_labels() %>%
  modify_caption("**Table 1. Multivariable Cox Regression Analysis**")
```

```{r}
# 1. Test the PH assumption using Schoenfeld residuals
ph_test <- cox.zph(cox_fit)

# Publication-ready plot of Schoenfeld residuals
ggcoxzph(ph_test, ggtheme = theme_bw(), font.main = 12)
```

```{r}

# 1. Create a proper 0/1 dummy variable (0 = Male, 1 = Female)
lung$sex_dummy <- lung$sex - 1

# 1. Handling Non-PH: Stratified Cox Model
cox_strat <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + strata(sex), data = lung)

# 2. Handling Non-PH: Time-varying coefficient
cox_time_var <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + factor(sex) + tt(sex_dummy), 
                      data = lung,
                      tt = function(x, t, ...) x * log(t))

# 3. Compare the original model with the time-varying model
tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(
    tbl_regression(cox_strat, exponentiate = TRUE),
    tbl_regression(cox_time_var, exponentiate = TRUE)
  ),
  tab_spanner = c("**Standard Cox**", "**Time-varying Cox**")
)
```

```{r}

# --- 0. Prepare 'mgus2' data ---
# Create a single time-to-first-event variable (etime)
# If no progression (pstat == 0), use follow-up time (futime). Otherwise, use progression time (ptime).
mgus2$etime <- ifelse(mgus2$pstat == 0, mgus2$futime, mgus2$ptime)

# Create a single event status variable
# 1 = PCM (Progression), 2 = Death without PCM, 0 = Censored
mgus2$event <- ifelse(mgus2$pstat == 1, 1, 
                      ifelse(mgus2$death == 1, 2, 0))

# Convert to factor with strict labels for tidycmprsk
mgus2$event_factor <- factor(mgus2$event, 0:2, c("Censored", "PCM", "Death"))

# --- 1. Publication-ready Cumulative Incidence Function (CIF) Plot ---
cuminc_fit <- cuminc(Surv(etime, event_factor) ~ sex, data = mgus2)

ggcuminc(cuminc_fit, outcome = c("PCM", "Death")) +
  add_confidence_interval() +
  add_risktable() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Cumulative Incidence of PCM and Death", x = "Months")

# --- 2. Cause-specific Cox (Etiology) ---
# For cause-specific hazard of PCM, we treat competing events (Death) as censored (0)
mgus2$status_pcm <- ifelse(mgus2$event == 1, 1, 0)
cs_cox <- coxph(Surv(etime, status_pcm) ~ age + sex, data = mgus2)

# --- 3. Fine-Gray Subdistribution Cox (Prediction) ---
# Fine-Gray natively handles competing risks using the factor variable we created
fg_cox <- crr(Surv(etime, event_factor) ~ age + sex, data = mgus2)

# --- 4. Side-by-side comparison table ---
tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(
    tbl_regression(cs_cox, exponentiate = TRUE),
    tbl_regression(fg_cox, exponentiate = TRUE)
  ),
  tab_spanner = c("**Cause-Specific**", "**Fine-Gray**")
)
```

```{r}
# Create a dummy dataset
set.seed(123)
dummy_data <- data.frame(
  id = 1:5,
  trt_start_time = c(NA, 50, 120, 30, NA), # NA means never treated
  fup_time = c(100, 200, 150, 80, 250),
  event = c(1, 0, 1, 1, 0)
)

# 1. Extended Cox (tmerge)
base_data <- tmerge(data1 = dummy_data, data2 = dummy_data, id = id, 
                    event = event(fup_time, event))

td_data <- tmerge(data1 = base_data, data2 = dummy_data, id = id, 
                  trt_status = tdc(trt_start_time))

td_cox <- coxph(Surv(tstart, tstop, event) ~ trt_status, data = td_data)

# 2. Landmark Analysis (Landmark = Day 50)
LM_time <- 50
lm_data <- subset(dummy_data, fup_time > LM_time)
lm_data$trt_at_LM <- ifelse(!is.na(lm_data$trt_start_time) & lm_data$trt_start_time <= LM_time, 1, 0)
lm_data$fup_from_LM <- lm_data$fup_time - LM_time

lm_cox <- coxph(Surv(fup_from_LM, event) ~ trt_at_LM, data = lm_data)

# 3. Landmark Survival Plot
lm_fit <- survfit(Surv(fup_from_LM, event) ~ trt_at_LM, data = lm_data)

ggsurvplot(
  lm_fit, 
  data = lm_data, 
  risk.table = TRUE,
  palette = c("#E7B800", "#2E9FDF"),
  ggtheme = theme_minimal(),
  title = "Landmark Survival (from Day 50)",
  legend.labs = c("Untreated at LM", "Treated at LM")
)


# 4. Present findings (Table)
tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(
    tbl_regression(td_cox, exponentiate = TRUE),
    tbl_regression(lm_cox, exponentiate = TRUE)
  ),
  tab_spanner = c("**Time-Dependent Cox**", "**Landmark (Day 50)**")
)



```

```{r}
# 1. Load data and define the transition matrix
data(ebmt3)
tmat <- trans.illdeath(c("Transplant", "Recovery", "Relapse/Death"))

# 2. Prepare data to long format
# The 'keep = "age"' argument ensures the factor variable isn't dropped
ms_data <- msprep(time = c(NA, "prtime", "rfstime"), 
                  status = c(NA, "prstat", "rfsstat"), 
                  data = ebmt3, 
                  trans = tmat,
                  keep = "age")

# 3. Expand the factor covariate
# Because 'age' has 3 levels (<=20, 20-40, >40), and '<=20' is the reference,
# R creates dummies for the other two levels across the 3 transitions.
ms_data <- expand.covs(ms_data, "age")

# 4. Fit the stratified Cox model
# We explicitly call the newly generated dummy columns for each transition
ms_cox <- coxph(Surv(Tstart, Tstop, status) ~ 
                  age20.40.1 + age.40.1 +  # Transition 1: Transplant -> Recovery
                  age20.40.2 + age.40.2 +  # Transition 2: Transplant -> Relapse/Death
                  age20.40.3 + age.40.3 +  # Transition 3: Recovery -> Relapse/Death
                  strata(trans), 
                data = ms_data)

# 5. Generate the publication-ready table
# exponentiate = TRUE gives us Hazard Ratios instead of log-hazards
tbl_regression(ms_cox, exponentiate = TRUE) %>% 
  modify_caption("**Multi-State Transitions: Impact of Age Category (Baseline: <=20)**")
```

```{r}
data(rats, package = "survival") 

# 1. Standard Cox (ignores clustering)
cox_naive <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ rx + sex, data = rats)

# 2. Mixed-Effects Cox (Gaussian frailty) using coxme package
cox_mixed <- coxme(Surv(time, status) ~ rx + sex + (1 | litter), data = rats)

# Combine for publication table
tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(
    tbl_regression(cox_naive, exponentiate = TRUE),
    tbl_regression(cox_mixed, exponentiate = TRUE)
  ),
  tab_spanner = c("**Naive (No Frailty)**", "**Mixed Effects (Frailty)**")
)

```

```{r}
# Setup data
data(colon)
colon_death <- subset(colon, etype == 2) # Filter to model death only

# Task 1: Fit the Cox Model
cox_colon <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ rx + age, data = colon_death)

# Task 2: Schoenfeld Residuals Test
test_colon <- cox.zph(cox_colon)
print(test_colon) # View p-values

# Task 3: Plot the residuals
ggcoxzph(test_colon, font.main = 12)
# Answer: The p-value for 'rx' < 0.05, and the plot shows a trend 
# line that isn't flat. The PH assumption is violated. The treatment effect 
# is stronger early on and wanes over time.

# Task 4 (Bonus): How to adjust?
# You could use a stratified model if rx was just a confounder, but since it's 
# the treatment of interest, you should use a time-varying coefficient tt(rx) 
# or split the time window (e.g., effect in year 1 vs. effect after year 1).

```

```{r}
# Task 1: Competing Risks with mgus2
# Create binary hemoglobin category
mgus2$hgb_cat <- ifelse(mgus2$hgb > 12, "> 12 g/dL", "<= 12 g/dL")
mgus2$event_factor <- factor(mgus2$ptime, 0:2, c("Censored", "PCM", "Death"))

# Calculate and plot CIF
ex2_cuminc <- cuminc(Surv(futime, event_factor) ~ hgb_cat, data = mgus2)

ggcuminc(ex2_cuminc, outcome = c("PCM", "Death")) +
  add_confidence_interval() +
  add_risktable() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "CIF by Hemoglobin Category", x = "Months")

# Task 2: Frailty Modeling with Kidney dataset
data(kidney, package = "survival")

# Fit Naive Cox
naive_kidney <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = kidney)

# Fit Mixed-Effects (Frailty) Cox using patient ID
mixed_kidney <- coxme(Surv(time, status) ~ sex + (1 | id), data = kidney)

# Compare outputs
tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(
    tbl_regression(naive_kidney, exponentiate = TRUE),
    tbl_regression(mixed_kidney, exponentiate = TRUE)
  ),
  tab_spanner = c("**Naive (Ignores repeated infections)**", "**Frailty (Accounts for Patient ID)**")
)
```
